narrative_blocks.header: Physician who became treasurer of Hamilton College
narrative_blocks.body: A physician in Massachusetts and New York, in 1831 he relocated to Clinton NY to become treasurer of Hamilton College — where his brother Sereno Edwards Dwight would serve as the college's third president two years later. His daughter-line married into the Hamilton academic community.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of Columbia Law School's founding dean
narrative_blocks.body: Two of his sons became major American scholars: Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight, an educator and author, and Theodore William Dwight, who founded the Columbia Law School and served as its first dean for over thirty years. The Dwight line of academic prominence ran through his household.

narrative_blocks.header: Physician who became treasurer of Hamilton College
narrative_blocks.body: A physician in Massachusetts and New York, in 1831 he relocated to Clinton NY to become treasurer of Hamilton College — where his brother Sereno Edwards Dwight would serve as the college's third president two years later. His daughter-line married into the Hamilton academic community.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of Columbia Law School's founding dean
narrative_blocks.body: Two of his sons became major American scholars: Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight, an educator and author, and Theodore William Dwight, who founded the Columbia Law School and served as its first dean for over thirty years. The Dwight line of academic prominence ran through his household.

narrative_blocks.header: Married as an invalid, expecting to die
narrative_blocks.body: Weakened by a severe cold made worse by 'too unremitted attention to some favorite tasks in embroidery she had imposed upon herself,' she could not sit up and coughed blood with any exertion. In spring 1815 she was married to physician Benjamin Woolsey Dwight at her uncle's house in South Hadley MA in her sick gown, expecting to live only a little while.

narrative_blocks.header: Recovered through her physician husband's care
narrative_blocks.body: Her new husband, a trained physician, applied vigorous medical means over several months and she recovered her strength enough to enjoy comfortable health for many years afterward. She lived another forty-six years and bore five children including the future founder of Columbia Law School Theodore William Dwight and the educator-genealogist Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Yale, Hamilton, and Columbia educators
narrative_blocks.body: Her six children spanned the New England intellectual elite: Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight (Yale Theology + genealogist), Theodore William Dwight (Columbia Law founder), Sophia (cousin marriage), Mary (Chicago Judge Anthony's first wife), Edward Woolsey (Wisconsin Greenback Party), and Elizabeth (Mary's widower's second wife).

narrative_blocks.header: Daughters' generation pioneered sister-replacement marriages
narrative_blocks.body: Three of her daughters' generation in the Dwight family practiced sister-replacement marriage as a stabilization strategy after tuberculosis-era widow-hood: Julia Dwight then Mary Woolsey Dwight to Dr. Lyman (1847+1855); her own daughter Mary then Elizabeth to Judge Anthony (1852+1865); cousin Lucy Waterman then Elizabeth Adams to Rev. Edward Strong Dwight (1849+1864).

cross_connections.display_label: Compiled the 1876 Talcott family genealogy of Hartford line
Compiled the 1909 Descendants of Thomas Hooker, two volumes

narrative_blocks.header: Author of the histories that document this database's
narrative_blocks.detail: After building and operating three iterations of Dwight's High School (Brooklyn 1846-1858, Clinton NY 1858-1863, New York City 1863-1867), Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight returned to Clinton NY and devoted himself to writing. His school at Brooklyn in 1846 was the first American school to offer German as a regular daily subject. As genealogical compiler he produced the definitive histories of the Dwight and Strong families — still the primary sources for both lines. He contributed to Bibliotheca Sacra, The New Englander, and the NY Genealogical Record. He briefly edited The Interior, a Presbyterian weekly in Chicago, but found it financially untenable and resigned after five months.
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight compiled the two-volume History of the Dwight Family and the two-volume History of the Strong Family — both primary sources for this database. His brother Theodore William Dwight founded Columbia Law School; his sister Mary Dwight married Judge Elliott Anthony of Chicago.

narrative_blocks.header: First American school to teach daily German, 1846
narrative_blocks.body: In 1846 he founded Dwight's High School in Brooklyn NY — a commercial and classical school of 120-160 boys that ran for twelve years. It is believed to be the first American school in which German was lifted to the plane of a regular daily study, taught alongside Latin and French to all pupils rather than as a dilettante elective.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote the family genealogies, two volumes each
narrative_blocks.body: He compiled and published the History of the Descendants of John Dwight of Dedham Mass (2 vols 1874) and the History of the Descendants of Elder John Strong of Northampton (2 vols 1871) — two of the foundational New England family genealogies cited across this tree, including the Major Timothy Dwight III Natchez accounts and the Strong-Hooker double-line marriages.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Joliet Illinois Congregational Church, 1844
narrative_blocks.body: Before his Brooklyn school years he founded the First Congregational Church of Joliet IL in 1844 and served as its pastor for two years. The church is now the Central Presbyterian Church of Joliet; he also briefly edited The Interior, a Presbyterian weekly out of Chicago, in 1872.

institutions.blurb: Hamilton College BA 1835; tutor at Hamilton 1839-1842; father served as Hamilton treasurer concurrently.

orbit_connections.name: Grover Cleveland (X00770) — taught by Elliott Anthony who studied under Dwight
orbit_connections.relationship: Anthony (who married Theodore's sister) taught Cleveland before studying law under Theodore Dwight

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Columbia Law School
narrative_blocks.detail: In 1858 Theodore William Dwight accepted an invitation to develop a law department at Columbia College. He was the sole professor until 1873 when it expanded into Columbia Law School. The "Dwight method" — memorization of treatises, practice drills, frequent moot courts — competed directly with the case method developed by Christopher Columbus Langdell at Harvard. Columbia Professor Peter Strauss described the contrast: "Where Dwight aimed to give a sound knowledge of the law to men of average ability, Harvard's case method aimed to give as much intellectual stimulation as possible to those who would become the profession's elite." In 1891 Dwight led faculty, students, and alumni out of Columbia in protest of trustees' attempt to convert to the case method, and co-founded New York Law School. A column on Columbia's College Walk at 116th and Amsterdam is dedicated to him.
narrative_blocks.body: Built Columbia's law department from scratch in 1858; sole professor for 15 years; dean until 1891. Led the faculty walkout over the Harvard "case method" controversy and co-founded New York Law School the same year.

narrative_blocks.header: Studied physics under Samuel F.B. Morse
narrative_blocks.detail: Theodore William Dwight graduated Hamilton College in 1840 as a member of Alpha Delta Phi, having studied physics under Samuel F.B. Morse (who the following year would demonstrate long-distance telegraphy). Beyond legal education, Dwight was a prominent prison reformer: he collaborated on A Report on Prisons and Reformatories in the United States and Canada (1867), served as president of the New York Prison Association, was delegate to the International Prison Congress at Stockholm in 1878, helped draft the bill establishing the Elmira Reformatory, and wrote the early report leading to the organization of the State Charities Aid Association. He also served as counsel for five Andover Theological Seminary professors charged with heresy in 1886.
narrative_blocks.body: At Hamilton College he studied under Samuel F.B. Morse and John William Draper. As a reformer he helped draft legislation for the Elmira Reformatory, served as president of the New York Prison Association, and attended the International Prison Congress in Stockholm (1878).

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Columbia Law School, 1858
narrative_blocks.body: Elected Professor of Municipal Law at Columbia College in 1858, he organized the Columbia Law School with thirty-five pupils in its first year and was made its Warden. Under his sole instruction the school grew to 425 students by 1874 — his style of recitation-and-lecture pedagogy with weekly moot court drew national attention.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded New York Law School, 1891
narrative_blocks.body: After 33 years at Columbia he resigned in 1891 and that same year founded New York Law School with several Columbia colleagues — making him a founder of two major American law schools. He died the following year in 1892 at his Clinton NY family home.

narrative_blocks.header: Won state law admitting law graduates to bar
narrative_blocks.body: Before Columbia he taught law at Hamilton 1846-1858 and procured passage of a New York statute admitting his Hamilton Law School graduates to practice on the basis of their diploma alone. When the Supreme Court declared the statute unconstitutional he won reversal at the Court of Appeals — the argument was published as a thick pamphlet by Hamilton's trustees.

institutions.blurb: Hamilton College BA 1840 (Alpha Delta Phi); tutor 1842-1846; Professor of Law, History, Civil Polity, and Political Economy 1846-1858; Dean of Hamilton Law School 1853.
Founder + first Warden of Columbia Law School 1858; grew school to 425 students by 1874
Founder 1891 — the year before his death

narrative_blocks.header: Family added the 'a' to Olmstead after move
narrative_blocks.body: Born Mary Bond Olmsted in Plainfield MA in 1823, daughter of Asa Olmsted. In the 1840s her family moved to Clinton NY where her father started using the surname with an added 'a' as 'Olmstead' to match other Clinton-area families. She married Theodore William Dwight in 1847 and bore three children before his Columbia Law career began.

landmarks.blurb: Wife of Edward Lasell Partridge; died 1907, two years before Bear Mountain State Park was established.

narrative_blocks.header: Died before her husband's Bear Mountain victory
narrative_blocks.body: Daughter of Theodore William Dwight founder of Columbia Law School, she married the NYC obstetrician Edward Lasell Partridge in 1884 at age 28. She died at Cornwall-on-Hudson NY in 1907 at fifty — two years before her husband's lobbying campaign succeeded in creating Bear Mountain State Park.

cross_connections.notes: HD1990 Sophia Dwight married her first cousin HD1982 John William Dwight, Jun 18, 1845.
cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both grandchildren of Yale president Timothy Dwight.

cross_connections.notes: HD1990 Sophia Dwight married her first cousin HD1982 John William Dwight, Jun 18, 1845.
cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both grandchildren of Yale president Timothy Dwight.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with husband John William Dwight

narrative_blocks.header: Cousin marriage to another Dwight
narrative_blocks.body: On 18 June 1845 — exactly the day she would die eighteen years later — she married her first cousin John William Dwight, son of her uncle Timothy Dwight Jr. and Clarissa Strong of New Haven. The marriage doubled her children's Hooker descent through two paths back to Pope Dwight and gave them the unusual 'Dwight Dwight' surname.

narrative_blocks.header: Six children, three lost before age four
narrative_blocks.body: She bore six children between 1849 and 1860: Mary Clare lived to 91; Dr. Edward Strong became a Massachusetts physician living to 87; and Emily Cecelia reached 30. Three sons died young — Henry Woolsey at 1, Frederic Louis at 3, and Louis Theodore at 2. She herself died at 45 in New Haven on her own 18th wedding anniversary.

cross_connections.notes: Both gen 7. HD1982 also doubly Hooker-descended through mother Clarissa Strong (HD0371). The Dwight family genealogy (written by HD1990's brother Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight) describes Sophia as a lady of "great personal beauty and sweetness of character."
cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both grandchildren of Yale president Timothy Dwight.

cross_connections.notes: Both gen 7. HD1982 also doubly Hooker-descended through mother Clarissa Strong (HD0371). The Dwight family genealogy (written by HD1990's brother Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight) describes Sophia as a lady of "great personal beauty and sweetness of character."
cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both grandchildren of Yale president Timothy Dwight.
A Strong cousin named his son Dwight

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with wife Sophia Dwight Dwight

narrative_blocks.header: His bride was also his cousin, Sophia Dwight
narrative_blocks.body: John William Dwight married Sophia Dwight in June 1845 — his first cousin, daughter of Dr. Benjamin Woolsey and Sophia (Strong) Dwight of Clinton, New York. Both were grandchildren of President Timothy Dwight of Yale; cousin marriage was legal and common in New England.

narrative_blocks.header: His saltpeter business fed the gunpowder trade
narrative_blocks.body: John William Dwight established himself in New Haven as a manufacturer of saltpetre — potassium nitrate, the primary oxidizer in gunpowder. He operated through the 1840s and into the Civil War decade, when demand for gunpowder ingredients reached its wartime peak.

career.location: New Haven, New Haven County, Connecticut
career.notes: Manufacturer of saltpetre (potassium nitrate) at New Haven CT. Per EH 1909 #2681.

narrative_blocks.header: Born May 1847; dead in Clinton, three months
narrative_blocks.body: Sophia Clarissa Dwight was born May 1, 1847 at New Haven and died August 21, 1847 in Clinton, New York — three and a half months old. She was the first of John William and Sophia Dwight's seven children.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Chicago lawyer Elliott Anthony, 1852
narrative_blocks.body: In July 1852 she married Elliott Anthony — a prominent Chicago lawyer who would later sit on the Illinois Circuit Court. They moved to Chicago where she bore four children. She died at 39 in Chicago in February 1864 and was buried at Graceland Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband later married her younger sister Elizabeth
narrative_blocks.body: After Mary's 1864 death her widower Elliott Anthony married her younger sister Elizabeth Dwight in January 1865 as his second wife; Elizabeth bore no children and devoted her life to raising Mary's four. The two Dwight sisters thus shared one Chicago lawyer husband across two marriages.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandmother of 4-time WWGA golf champion
narrative_blocks.body: Through her son Charles Elliott Anthony who married Mary P. Green in Chicago 1879, she became grandmother of Mary Elizabeth 'Bessie' Anthony Horne — the 4-time consecutive Women's Western Golf Association champion 1901-1904. Bessie's 1903 marriage to Pittsburgh Pictorialist photographer Bernard Shea Horne Sr extended the Dwight-Anthony line into Pittsburgh's industrial dynasty.

cross_connections.display_label: Anthony read law with him after Hamilton 1850 per Wikipedia
Both Republican Party founders (Anthony IL; Ashmun MA 1860 chair)
Both Republican Party founders (Anthony IL; Bowles MA 1855)
was his pupil at Clinton Academy before the presidency

orbit_connections.name: Grover Cleveland (X00770) — his pupil at Clinton Academy
orbit_connections.relationship: Anthony taught Cleveland at Clinton Academy, Clinton NY, circa 1851
orbit_connections.notes: Cleveland was later the 22nd and 24th President of the United States

notable.notable_blurb: Illinois lawyer and judge active in the founding

burial.plot_notes: Graceland Cemetery, Chicago IL — family plot of 12 burials he purchased

education.notes: Read law afterward with Timothy Dwight V per Wikipedia (chronological issue: Tim V was 22 + theology tutor; more plausibly with Theodore William Dwight HD1989, his Hamilton Law professor 1846-58)

career.notes: Admitted NY bar May 1851; one year Sterling IL; returned East July 1852 to marry Mary Dwight; settled Chicago November 1852. Published a digest of the Illinois Reports within two years (with wife's aid). Elected Chicago City Attorney then Corporation Counsel.

Until consolidation with Chicago and North Western Railway 1864. Then employed by non-consenting stockholders+bondholders in litigation to break up consolidation. Argued before Justice David Davis (US Supreme Court) and Samuel Hubbel Treat Jr. (Springfield); won for his clients. Published book on railway consolidation 1865.
Under Mayor Monroe Heath; part of the great reform movement with Thomas Hoyne.
Elected November 1880 by a large majority. Identified with many of Chicago's public improvements — graded streets, waterworks, public parks, public library, cemeteries.

narrative_blocks.header: Read law in Dwight family, married in
narrative_blocks.body: After graduating Hamilton with high honors in 1850 he read law in the Dwight family orbit (Theodore William Dwight was his Hamilton law professor). In May 1851 he was admitted to the New York bar; in July 1852 he married Mary Dwight — granddaughter of Timothy Dwight IV and sister of the future 12th Yale president Timothy Dwight V.

narrative_blocks.header: Republican Party founder for over a quarter century
narrative_blocks.body: Raised a Quaker, he took part in the formation of the Republican Party and led the organization in Illinois for more than 25 years. He was a delegate to the 1880 Republican National Convention and aided in the nomination of James A. Garfield for President.

narrative_blocks.header: Won landmark railroad consolidation case
narrative_blocks.body: As General Solicitor of the Galena and Chicago Union Railroad 1858-1864 he argued the major case challenging the railroad's consolidation with the Chicago and North Western. The case went before Justice David Davis of the U.S. Supreme Court and Samuel Hubbel Treat Jr. of Springfield IL; Anthony won for his non-consenting stockholder clients and published a book on railway consolidation in 1865.

narrative_blocks.header: Married two Dwight sisters, 13 years apart
narrative_blocks.body: On 14 July 1852 he married Mary Dwight, 27, in Catskill NY — they had four children and lived in Chicago. Mary died at 39 in February 1864. On 11 January 1865 he married her younger sister Elizabeth Dwight, 33 — she bore no children and devoted her life to raising Mary's four before her own death at 38 in 1870.

narrative_blocks.header: He taught at Clinton Academy before the law
narrative_blocks.body: Before he turned to law, Anthony taught at Clinton Academy in upstate New York. One of his pupils was a boy named Grover Cleveland, the future president.

narrative_blocks.header: Phillips Academy Andover class of 1875
narrative_blocks.body: After his uncle's father-in-law Rev. John Ripley Adams had served as a teacher there in 1821-22, and 40 years after John Adams's 22-year principalship ended, he graduated Phillips Academy Andover in 1875 — first alphabetically on his class list. He went on to Union College of Law Chicago in 1878.

narrative_blocks.header: Chicago Fire witness at fifteen
narrative_blocks.body: He was fifteen when the Great Chicago Fire swept the city 8-11 October 1871. His father Judge Elliott Anthony saved approximately 1,500 books by burying them in his garden; most of his illustrated books and encyclopedias were burned. He carried these memories into his own 60-year Chicago legal career.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of America's first 4-time golf champion
narrative_blocks.body: He married Mary P. Green in September 1879 in Chicago; they had four daughters. Eldest Bessie won the Women's Western Golf Association championship four years in a row 1901-1904 — making her one of the most dominant early American women's sports figures before her 1912 death at 32.

cross_connections.display_label: Second cousin: fellow Pope Dwight grandchild + Phillips Academy alumnus

institutions.notes: Class of 1875; first alphabetically on class list.

education.notes: First alphabetically on class list. ~40 years after John Adams's principalship ended; ~50 years after his uncle Rev. Edward Strong Dwight's father-in-law Civil War chaplain John Ripley Adams taught there.

narrative_blocks.header: Chicago Polyclinic dermatology professor
narrative_blocks.body: Son of Judge Elliott Anthony, he became a Chicago physician and served as professor of dermatology at both the Chicago Polyclinic and Rush Medical Colleges. He was physician to the Children's Memorial Hospital and died at his home, 1251 Dearborn Avenue, age 51 in July 1912.

notable.notable_blurb: American farmer, frontier pioneer, and politician

narrative_blocks.header: Wisconsin frontier farmer for fifty-six years
narrative_blocks.body: At twenty-one he married Elizabeth Foote and moved to Spring Prairie WI in Walworth County to farm. He spent the rest of his life — 56 years — as a Wisconsin farmer, far from the Dwight family's Yale-Hamilton-Columbia intellectual axis his brothers Theodore William and Benjamin Woodbridge occupied.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived four siblings in the Wisconsin countryside
narrative_blocks.body: By the time he died at 76 in 1904 he had outlived all four of his siblings — Sophia (1863), Mary (1864), Theodore William (1892), and Benjamin Woodbridge (1889). He was buried in Oregon, Dane County WI, far from the family plot at Grove Street and Sunset Hill Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Whaling schooner at fifteen, year on Atlantic
narrative_blocks.body: At fifteen he left his father's home and went to Boston, where he signed up on the whaling schooner Council and spent a year sailing the Atlantic Ocean. He returned to Boston and went to Clinton NY to work on a farm — the start of a 60-year agricultural career across three states.

narrative_blocks.header: Wisconsin State Assembly Republican, 1860
narrative_blocks.body: He was active in the Republican Party and held various offices, culminating in his election to a single one-year term in the Wisconsin State Assembly in 1860 — Lincoln's election year. Dane County lost a seat in the subsequent assembly and he did not return to elected office for two decades.

narrative_blocks.header: Greenback Party presidential elector, 1880
narrative_blocks.body: After the Civil War he spent a great deal of time studying financial questions. The result was a political pivot: in 1880 he served as a United States Presidential elector for the Greenback Party — a populist third party advocating greenback paper currency and against the gold standard. His brothers Theodore William and Benjamin Woodbridge remained Republican.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her late sister's widower, 1865
narrative_blocks.body: Eleven months after her sister Mary Dwight Anthony died at 39 in Chicago (February 1864), she married Mary's widower Judge Elliott Anthony in January 1865 as his second wife. She bore no children; she devoted her life to raising Mary's four. She died at 38 in Chicago five years later in June 1870.

narrative_blocks.header: Of indifferent health, but great vivacity and energy
narrative_blocks.body: Per the Dwight family history written by her brother Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight: 'While having always but very indifferent health, she was remarkable for her great vivacity and energy at all times, and was most earnestly religious in all her plans and purposes of life... Death was to her but going home.' She spent her unmarried years devoted to her mother's care.

cross_connections.notes: No other family has provided three presidents of Yale. Timothy Dwight College at Yale is named for both the 8th and 12th presidents.
No other family has provided three presidents of Yale. Timothy Dwight College at Yale is named for both the 8th and 12th presidents.

cross_connections.display_label: Called Yale colleague James Luce Kingsley 'the American Addison'

cross_connections.description: Talcott-side descendant given middle name 'Dwight' in 1855, likely after Pope Dwight (8th Yale president); a second Timothy Dwight Hobart (TD0131 b.1826) carries the same name pattern.

narrative_blocks.header: He made Yale the nation’s largest college
narrative_blocks.body: As Yale’s eighth president from 1795 to 1817 he hired Benjamin Silliman as its first chemistry professor — later called the patriarch of American science — and grew Yale into the largest college in the country. His revival sermons there helped touch off the Second Great Awakening.

narrative_blocks.header: He argued for independence a year early
narrative_blocks.body: In July 1775, a full year before the Declaration, he was openly urging the colonies to declare independence — by his own account using the very arguments later adopted — while many around him were still fearful or opposed.

narrative_blocks.header: He wrote America’s first epic poem
narrative_blocks.body: In 1785 he published “The Conquest of Canaan,” an eleven-book biblical epic dedicated to George Washington and reckoned the first epic poem written in America. He was a leading “Hartford Wit” alongside John Trumbull, Joel Barlow, and Noah Webster.

narrative_blocks.header: A mission in Arkansas still bears his name
narrative_blocks.body: He helped found the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions in 1810; a decade later, in 1820, its Dwight Mission to the Cherokee in Arkansas was named in his honor. As a Continental Army chaplain in the Revolution he had written the patriotic song “Columbia” for his troops.

narrative_blocks.header: Stolen pears taught him the eighth commandment
narrative_blocks.body: As a small boy he took pears from a neighbor’s yard with older schoolmates; his mother told him it broke the commandment against stealing. He returned them in tears and refused to keep them even when the owner offered — a story he later preached to his Yale congregation.

narrative_blocks.header: He wrote a war anthem as army chaplain
narrative_blocks.body: While serving as a chaplain in the Continental Army, Timothy Dwight wrote the patriotic song "Columbia." Set to music, it became one of the young republic's stirring anthems.

artworks.blurb: John Trumbull oil portrait, painted 1817 — year of Pope Dwight's death.

institutions.institution_blurb: Founding figure; Dwight Mission named in his honor.

institutions.blurb: Taught at Hopkins Grammar School in New Haven after his 1769 Yale graduation, before becoming a Yale Tutor.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with wife Mary Woolsey, son James Dwight III, granddaughter Aurelia, grandson Tim V

education.notes: Entered Yale at age 13; graduated top of

career.notes: pastor; ran a noted boys’ academy

parents.father_name: Benjamin Woolsey of Long Island NY — was Maj. Timothy Dwight III's Yale classmate, roommate, and intimate friend
parents.father_research_notes: Mary's father Benjamin Woolsey predeceased the Dwight marriage. Earlier tree data incorrectly set father_id=H00914 (her HUSBAND Pope Dwight). Corrected Stage 6Y-161.

bio.extra_names: Sometimes Margaret (EH 914 calls her Margaret; FAG and consensus sources Mary; per Sam, the same person — Mary as nickname for Margaret)

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with husband Pope Dwight

narrative_blocks.header: Brother and sister-in-law cemented Woolsey-Dwight ties
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Benjamin Woolsey had been Maj. Timothy Dwight III's Yale classmate, roommate, and intimate friend — the Woolsey-Dwight connection preceded her own 1777 marriage to Pope Dwight. Her brother William Walter Woolsey later married Elizabeth Dwight, sister of her husband Pope Dwight, doubling the family link in the next generation.

burial.plot_notes: Unknown grave near Natchez, Adams County, Mississippi — died in the wilderness 1777, body location never recorded

career.notes: Merchant in Northampton MA. Substantial family business.
Judge 1758-1774. Took oath of fealty to British crown on assuming office — held this oath binding through the Revolution and refused to revoke it.

narrative_blocks.header: Loyalist judge who would not break his oath
narrative_blocks.body: He had sworn fealty to the British crown in 1758 on becoming judge of the court of common pleas, and held that oath binding when the Revolution came. His Northampton family suffered increasing community ostracism as the war approached; one biographer called him 'a Loyalist on Christian principle, and yet thoroughly patriotic in his feelings.'

narrative_blocks.header: Took two sons to a Mississippi colony, 1776
narrative_blocks.body: Disillusioned with revolutionary Northampton, in May 1776 he sailed from Middletown CT with two of his sons — Sereno and Jonathan — toward a British land grant at Natchez Mississippi. They were accompanied by the Phelps and Lyman families and the Rev Mr Smith's household; within a year he and Mrs Lyman would both be dead at Natchez of fever.

narrative_blocks.header: Died in the wilderness, body never recovered
narrative_blocks.body: He died at Natchez Mississippi in June 1777 at age fifty in the wilderness, without trial or recorded grave. News of his death traveled slowly back to Northampton; his son Timothy Dwight IV left the Continental Army in 1778 to return home as the oldest surviving male and take charge of the family's affairs.

narrative_blocks.header: Carried tiny wife on his palm, arm's length
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Mary Edwards Dwight was as small as he was large — he sometimes carried her around the room on his open palm held out at arm's length. She bore him thirteen children, eight of them sons, all noted for fine physical forms — the smallest son Cecil was five-foot-eight-and-a-half and weighed two hundred pounds.

cross_connections.display_label: Refused his Northampton congregation, took communion twelve miles away instead.

narrative_blocks.header: Married at sixteen as Edwards left for Stockbridge
narrative_blocks.body: Her November 1750 wedding to Major Timothy Dwight came two months before Jonathan Edwards moved his family to the Stockbridge mission. The marriage was permitted because the family was about to be separated.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Yale's eighth president Timothy Dwight
narrative_blocks.body: Her son Timothy Dwight IV led Yale College from 1795 to 1817. He shaped a generation of New England intellectual life from his grandfather Edwards's old territory.

narrative_blocks.header: Refused her pew when Hooker succeeded her father
narrative_blocks.body: When the distant Thomas Hooker descendant Rev. John Hooker became Northampton's pastor after Edwards's dismissal, she placed her own chair in the vestibule below the belfry. On communion Sabbaths she rode twelve miles on a pillion behind her son to take communion at another church.

narrative_blocks.header: Heard a year later her husband had died
narrative_blocks.body: Major Timothy Dwight III died at Natchez, Mississippi in 1777 during a southern trip. The news did not reach her at Northampton until a full year after his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband could carry her on his open palm
narrative_blocks.body: She was unusually small in stature and was described in old age as 'opinionated and self-willed.' Patriot rabble burned her fields and stole her livestock during the Revolution because of her husband's Tory sympathies.

INST027.name: Hamilton College
INST027.notes: Liberal arts college in Clinton, Oneida County NY. Sereno Edwards Dwight (HD1970) served as 3rd President 1833-1835 (his career cut short by mercury poisoning). Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight (HD1988) graduated 1835; was tutor 1839-1842. Theodore William Dwight (HD1989) graduated 1840; taught 1842-1858; Dean of Hamilton Law School 1853 before moving to Columbia. Elliott Anthony (husband of HD1991) studied law here under Theodore William Dwight. Benjamin Woolsey Dwight (HD1967) was treasurer of Hamilton College. Asahel Clark Kendrick (I00943) graduated A.B. 1831 and immediately joined the faculty as a tutor, beginning his career in classical languages before moving to Madison University (Colgate).
